[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

[IMAGE:1]The illustration on the left?  Once again, that's the Monas Hieroglyphica of Dr. John Dee, the great Elizabethan astrologer, alchemist, occultist, and mage. Another version of what it means is over here to the right, which is part of a page from Dee's book: this diagram shows how the emblems of the planets are extracted from the Monad. Yes, there are two emblems for Mercury, the familiar one and a different one; these are the wingless and winged dragons of alchemical lore. If you want to try your hand at solving the riddle of the Monad's deeper meaning, here you go. 

[IMAGE:2]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:3]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: gods and psychotherapy
comments.content:
Which gods and planets should someone appeal to for assistance with success in therapy?
Mythology shows gods behaving in ways that don't fit with what healthy post-therapy humans in the 21st century are supposed to do, but maybe they are still relevant anyway. Is it necessary to square that?
Does it depend what the therapy is for? But a lot of therapy is about relationships to one extent or another. This is anyway. So maybe Venus/Aphrodite? I feel like it's more of a Hermes/Odin exercise, but perhaps that's because I find it hard to look into the right stuff, and why I'm doing the therapy.

replies.title: Re: gods and psychotherapy
replies.content: It depends entirely on what the therapy is for and what kind of approach is used. You might consider journaling, meditation, and divination around that -- you will learn a great dela more that way than you expect...

replies.title: Re: gods and psychotherapy
replies.content:
I don't do divination but I've spent a lot of time thinking and writing about the issues over the years. I feel like Odin is a sort of goal, like the wisdom to aspire to once it's done. (I've done a lot of therapy successfully for other things and I understand the feeling I mean.)

The process is essentially making part of myself grow up, that didn't because of various issues with caregivers. Part of me still feels like a small kid and it's a nuisance to me. So maybe mother goddesses as well? That feels like it makes sense. Like Hera? Are there any goddesses who were basically good mothers in mythology? (I am primarily a Greco Roman pagan but I also have some interest in Norse, willing to consider others though I prefer a well-established ancient mythology)

Which comes to another question: for deities who don't have obvious days, are there systems or tables that show which days are best to appeal to them? Or can you do it equally well any time? With the exception of perhaps a handful of optimal feast days through the year?

replies.title: Re: gods and psychotherapy
replies.content:
Demeter's the Greek goddess who comes first to mind as an exccellent mother -- think of her relationship with Persephone. Leto, the mother of Apollo and Artemis, would also be a fine choice. In your place I'd invoke those two and also Athena, who's not a mother goddess but who helps heroes grow up and become wise and strong.

If they're not planetary deities you can invoke them at any time.

replies.title: Re: gods and psychotherapy
replies.content:
Always keep in mind that the mythology says more about the culture and the people who recorded the stories rather than the gods themselves.

Which divinity you appeal to depends on what kind of healing or therapy you are attempting. Hygieia, for example, is the Greek goddess associated with preventing illness, hence the modern word hygiene. While Panacaea, said to be the sister of Hygieia, is connected with curative medicines. Other cultures have their own healing spirits and divinities. Japan has a kami named Sukunabikona associated with healing. He invented saki and helps in healing with both humans and animals.

The emphasis with traditional divinities is healing of physical ailments rather than emotional ones but in these modern times I think the gods will be flexible and accommodating. JMG has already suggested journaling and meditating so all you can really do is experiment and see who's most responsive to your needs.

JLfromNH/Psychedelic Felicitous Turtle

comments.title: Tony Hawk
comments.content:
Congratulations on the new book & cookbook publication!

Skateboard celebrity Tony Hawk recently made waves by selling his soul to a bottled water company. Allegedly he signed a contract where the fine print stated that his soul now belongs to Liquid Death Water. He allowed his blood to be drawn in order to create paint for a batch of limited edition skateboards.

https://www.benzinga.com/news/21/08/22645379/tony-hawk-trending-for-selling-his-soul-what-investors-should-know

Hawk is doing everything he can (as you can see in the article photo) to express his own incredulity. He doesn't seem to take the sale of his soul seriously.

Obviously we can only guess what the repercussions of his actions will be, but do you think he'll have to pay the piper in any significant way because of this stunt? To whom did he sell his soul, if anyone? Does his disbelief insulate him from consequences?

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: A teacher of mine used to say, "It doesn't matter whether or not you believe in demons. They believe in you." His disbelief is irrelevant, just as it would be if he disbelieved in gravity. In the usual way of things, he'll be fine until he dies, but to judge by the traditional lore, his death may not be as far off as he imagines.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: Wow. Is there anything he can do to fix it, supposing he figures out something is wrong?  I highly doubt he realizes he is in any trouble at all.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: Unless the company sells it back, he's screwed.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: So once you sell your soul, that's it? There's no way back?

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: It's sometimes possible for a very close relationship with a deity to resolve it, but even so there's going to be karma to work through.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content:
One possible out is that since he made the contract with a US corporation, the contract would fall under US law, and I'm pretty sure selling your soul would run afoul of the 13th amendment if not something else. So the contract may well be ruled null and void.

Note: This is a best-case scenario. Don't sell your soul expecting to get off the hook on a technicality. That's how they get you. Also, I'd imagine it'd be considered fraud if you do it intentionally.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: That's actually an interesting question.  Under American law, usually both sides need some kind of consideration (each party to the contract is giving up something and getting something) in order for a contract to be binding.  There are other ways in which a contract, or an agreement/promise, can be enforced: things like reliance, promissory estoppel, etc.  Most of this would probably favor Mr. Hawk (if one can imagine a breach of contract resulting in a lawsuit), though I'm not sure if this kind of bizarre contract would be subject to the laws of a human jurisdiction.  Seems to be a few pay grades above that level.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: As a final thought (at least for now): whether or not Mr. Hawk's soul would be sufficient consideration (from an American legal standpoint) might hinge on whether he believes he has a soul AND whether the corporation believes in the existence of a soul.  If both do, and Mr. Hawk's was given something in exchange (a nice sum of cash or whatever), the contract could potentially be legally binding.  A court hearing a potential dispute isn't likely to touch on the actual existence of the soul with a ten foot pole.  As far as the Thirteenth Amendment, I can make a contract that sells my labor and it would be legally binding.  The Thirteenth Amendment does not preclude such a thing.  In fact, employment contracts are perfectly legal and quite common.  Given that, selling one's soul wouldn't seem to necessarily fall under slavery or forced servitude.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content:
Thank you for your responses!

Two things occur to me: first, courts - especially American courts - have been willing to take claims involving the supernatural seriously. For example, I learned recently about a man who tried to sue the devil. His suit was dismissed partly on the grounds that the devil, if he exists, is a foreign prince and so might be subject to immunity.

Second, in many traditions, the soul is not merely part of you, but is you. In which case selling your soul is indeed equivalent to selling yourself into slavery.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content:
Related to your second point, an American court might also see the agreement as so one-sided that the interests of justice require that it be invalidated. I can agree to a term of service in a contract (think of a military enlistment) that is quite binding, with few escape clauses. But selling one's whole being might be a tad too far, regardless of what Mr. Hawk got in return.

As stupid as his actions were, this has proven to be a very interesting, thought provoking incident.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content:
Another thought: also remember that once you're dead, the US courts are presumably not going to be able to help you.

Again, just don't sell your soul to anyone or anything. Just… don't.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: True! At that point your soul is no longer subject to US law, and -- ahem -- it may be someplace where the laws are slanted in favor of the purchaser...

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: Umm... what's the traditional lore say in 25 words or less?  Pop culture suggests a very short life and a bad end after that...

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: That's about right. His soul is now someone else's property, and if corporations are as demonic in nature as they certainly seem to be, he's going to have a very messy time of it in the afterlife. Not for eternity, mind you, but for a good long time.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content:
OK... a miserable go between lives plus supertankers full of karmic payback for no-shows for multiple lives while being miserable equals bad idea. Even an atheist could deduce this via Jung or New Thought...
Anyway - what does the purchaser of a soul want with it in the first place?

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: A chew toy...

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: That’s so sad. I really looked up to him when I was younger.

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content:

Unfortunately, those days were spent in the edgier end of 90's and early 00's culture that was still rebelling against the everything-is-of-the-devil religious conservatism of the 80's — something captured by the brand and its mascot.

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Tony Hawk
replies.content: I don't think all corporations are demonic, but I think it's quite possible for a corporation to be possessed by a demon. (It's a legal person, after all!) The thing to look for is bizarre evil that corresponds to the behavior of demons in traditional lore. Most bottled water companies don't buy people's souls or wallow in the imagery of evil...

comments.title: Tracks in Space, DMH Meditation, Occult Philosophy, Neoplatonists, Fiction, GD vs DMH

1. Many times, you've stressed the importance of following rituals exactly as-is in order to follow the tracks laid down in space by previous practitioners of that ritual (among other reasons, like safety and efficacy). Is this something that happens more on a scale rather than a binary? What I have in mind is performing the SOP as written in the DMH - some steps are very precisely defined, but others call on you to customize, such as which deities to invoke. My guess would be that it's something like "following the overall structure and only changing the parts that the book says to customize will get you most of the benefit, but you might find things even easier if you follow the exact wording that decades of druids have". Is that roughly right? Either way, I'm sure it's a fine theme for meditation!

2. The CGD gives fairly specific guidance on what to meditate on and for how long. Unless I've grossly misread, the DMH is much broader in guidance on themes for meditation and for how many days to use them. Is this simply another instance of the greater flexibility of the DMH vs GD approaches, or is there some other reason?
a. As a request for some specific guidance, I've been spending 3 weeks learning each gate of the SOP, and I've paired that with 3 days meditating on the element and its correspondences, then 3 days for each few from the corresponding aicme, and then 3 more days on the element. Does that seem like a reasonable course of meditation?

replies.content:
4. Possibly related: any recommendations on reading order for Neo-Platonist works, especially if interested in the theology of Proclus and Plotinus?

This has been my major focus in study and meditation for the last year and a half or so, and I've made a great deal of progress in that time-- after about 5 years of attempting to read Platonic texts and giving up after the first page of incomprehensible sentences in Plotinus or Iamblichus. If I may make a couple of recommendations--

The first difficulty you're going to face is that getting into Platonic philosophy is made extra difficult by the problem of translation. If you read Plato's Republic, for example, you'll hear about how the soul has 3 components, called "Reason" or "Mind," "Spirit," and "Appetite." And if you take these words as meaning what they usually do in English, you will be lead down a completely wrong path and you will be utterly at a loss when it comes to later thinkers like Plotinus.

So I recommend two things. First, make sure that the translations that you get include a glossary, which tells you what Greek word is being rendered by what English word, and what the Greek word actually means. The word sometimes translated as "reason," for example, is actually "nous." It means much more than reason-- and other others won't translate it as "reason" but, rather, as "Intellect," "Divine Mind," or even "Angelic Mind." If you know what nous means, you'll be able to identify it in any of its later English disguises and know what is actually being discussed.

Second, even with a glossary, it is very helpful to have a guide. Fortuntaely for you, one is now available. If you go to YouTube, you'll find a whole series of lectures done in the 90s by a philosopher named Pierre Grimes. Look for "Pierre Grimes Philosophical Research Society." Grimes has lectures on many of Plato's most important dialogues as well as his predecessors like Heraclitus, Anaximander, Thales and so on and his successors including Plotinus and Proclus. (Not Iamblichus, who was still largely ignored in the 1990s.) I've found that it was helpful, when approaching any particular text, to start by listening to Grimes's talk on it.

As for reading order, it's worth noting that there was a traditional order for reading Plato's dialogues in Iamblichus's school. It went like so:

Alcibiades,
Gorgias,
Phaedo,
Cratylus,
Theaetetus,
Sophist,
Statesman,
Phaedrus,
Symposium,
Philebus,

which are then crowned with Timaeus and Parmenides.

You'll notice that the Republic is absent. I would recommend actually starting with the Republic, with Grimes's lectures as a guide. That will do a good job of immersing you in Platonic thought, and then you can tackle the remaining dialogues one at a time. like JMG, I'd suggest moving in order from Plotinus to Iamblichus to Proclus, with one exception-- I started with Proclus's Elements of Theology, and you might want to as well. It's much shorter than the Enneads or than anything else that Proclus ever wrote, and provides a very helpful summary of Platonic thinking in general.

comments.title: The Moment?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I’ve been in a state of somewhat chaos for a while. And am curious about your opinion on something. I often feel I get two kinds of advice on how to remedy my poverty and general lack of direction. One is to “stop navel gazing and make a plan and stick to if”. The other is “forget your ego and it’s big plans, get right with God and/or Spirit and be in moment”.

Sometimes it seems I’ve tried both and not succeeded with either. I suppose for me right now the first idea is to commit to advancing from hostess in a negative atmosphere to server somewhere ok so I can make money to control my debts, retrain as a massage therapist and energy worker, pursue my interests in daoist alchemy, psychedelics, and philosophy, and potentially move to Brazil to be with a love interest. And the other just working at the restaurant as if it were an interest, forgetting the other stuff to be more grounded, pushing myself to “stop being offensive and negative” eg less sugar skulls, black metal, and vampire movies and breaking it off with the guy in Brazil because he was dating someone before me.

I know you’re not a psychedelics fan but I also wanted to ask your opinion about a vision I had on ayahuasca, whether what I saw is “a thing” or not, as literal truth vs metaphorical (vs only partly true). Overall I ended up regretting who I drank with so I don’t want to discuss with them.

The vision represented myself as broke and anxious (true) and reacting with this soulless compulsion of seeking male attention by promising “something extra” either sexually or in terms of things like magic, hypnosis that are “exciting”, and then being “screwed over” and wallowing in poverty and self pity.

The part I see is psychologically it’s true I compulsively seek attention rather than take practical action. Especially male but this last year I’ve attracted a variety of both males and butch lesbians telling me what to do, as well as harsh gurus of both sexes. That my desperation keeps attracting power trippers. I’ve been working on the Order of the Octagon stuff and feels it’s helping me, that I’ve unraveled due to feelings of shame, blame, guilt, etc, gone from being a constipated depressed IT cynic, obsessed with the idea of being evil rather than reconcile with my controlling mother and “being like the normies” to becoming hysterical. Basically I think I need to strike a middle path between defiance and repentance, and fulfill myself (as best i can burdened by debt)

What I wonder is… after I had this vision I suddenly had this fear… is having an attraction to kinky / submissive sexuality really literally the cause of all my life problems? When I was young, my crush was my friends religious father who left his porn lying around and I’ve had on and off an issue with that and attraction to the kind of sex you see in porn. I’ve always been embarrassed and conflicted about it, but is it really likely that having BDSM type turn ons as a woman equates to being a misogynist ruining your own life and that of other women? (I half think the facilitator was just yucked out by me and that somehow skewed the vision)

replies.title: Re: The Moment?
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, the two pieces of advice you've been given are each good for some people and really bad for others. If you spend all your time wallowing in your emotions, making a plan and sticking to it can help; if your life is rigid to the point of rigor mortis, letting go and being in the moment can help.

On the other hand, if you're in the middle of a chaotic transformational process, neither one is good advice, because you're not in a situation where you can do either one effectively. What works in that sort of situation is to focus on getting your head on straight, and manage as best you can in your outward life for the time being.

Since we've never met, I can't tell you if the vision you had is correct, but I know a lot of women who get caught in that kind of self-defeating process, so it may well be the case. As for the BDSM issue, nope. Many women -- not all, not necessarily even most, but many -- have a strong submissive side when it comes to sexuality (read any bodice ripper romance that's sold at least a million copies if you want proof of this), and that remains true and normal even though it's not socially acceptable at the moment. (Your facilitator was just yucking out.) In the 0/8 work of the Octagon Society, as you work on accepting other things, you'll need to work on accepting yourself, including your sexuality.

replies.title: Re: The Moment?
replies.content: Thanks.  Your words of wisdom ring very true.

replies.title: Re: The Moment?
replies.content: Personally, I think the attraction to kink and submission is a symptom of your life problems, not the cause of them. Address your problems and the attraction may go away.

replies.title: (no subject)

Excellent! Yes, there's a number code hardwired into the Ogham. The ancient Celts counted in 20s -- that's why in France they still say quatre vingt, "four twenties," for eighty -- and so the five aicme are respectively 1-5, 6-10, 11-15, and 16-20.

2. I'm currently working through a classical GD system of self-initiation. Admittedly, I've been going through it a bit more slowly than usual (1-2 years each grade). I notice that with eerie regularity, after I've been on a given grade for a while, certain developments will take place where I am temporarily out of/unable to work, essentially forcing me to finish up the grade coursework. Is this a known phenomenon among initiates? Is it something to be concerned about? Anyone else experience this as well?

3. Lastly, not so much a question posed specifically to JMG but moreso the commentariat as it's not magic-related: I'm looking to prepare for the coming months by stocking up on a high quantity of dry food (oats, beans, lentils etc.). I'm looking for a healthy way to store these things long-term that won't break the bank. Does anyone have any suggestions?

Thanks for your help,
Zach

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Treat the word "day" as meaning the period from when you get up to when you go to bed. It's less confusing that way.

2) Your higher self is telling you to get a move on! Yes, it's fairly common.

3) If you can find a store that sells to preppers, they fairly often have the facilities to load 5 gallon buckets with food and then fill the air space with CO2 or another inert gas.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Regarding [3]: I have long used mason jars and a vacuum pump to seal up dry goods for the long haul. (They sell vacuum pump adapters for mason jar lids, and these can easily be hooked up to any pump. I use a hand pump made for emptying brake fluid lines. The total setup was around $20. Mason jars are a little pricey, but we use them for canning, winemaking, and cups anyway, so always have lots on hand.)

replies.title: Food storage
replies.content:
5 gal (and sometimes 3 or 4 gal) plastic pails with lids can often be sourced from supermarket bakeries for free, if you're willing to clean out the industrial frosting residue. Just ask the staff, I've collected many many buckets this way, sometimes they'll even clean them for you. There are different lid styles, some better than others for long term storage. I use the less awesome lidded ones for making sauerkraut, brewing beer, storing cat food, as planters in the garden, and as utility buckets. Really, can you ever have enough buckets? There are also aftermarket lids available.

Then either seal you dry goods inside the bucket in some sort of mylar bag, use a chunk of dry ice, or just go the CO2 route JMG described. There is a vast prepper literature available on this subject via your favorite search engine.

comments.title: The Plutonian 90's
comments.content:
Related to my comment on Tony Hawk and the 90's edgelord reaction to the 80's everything-is-evil conservatism:

Being an older Millennial (the part of the generation sometimes known as "30 year old Boomers" for a variety of good reasons), I have fond memories of the 90's, but I can see now that there was a really dark underbelly to that decade: politically (e.g. Bill Clinton), economically (e.g. the fake prosperity of the tech bubble), and culturally.

There was a reaction to the stuffy and hysterical conservatisms of the 80's, which had included the Satanic panic. It seems to me that this is when you start really seeing media that revels in edginess and being eeeeeeviiiillll start creeping in from the subcultures that had previously contained them. (Part of this was an interest in darkly-surreal imagery that resembles the sort of hodge-podge of parts that people report in visions of demons.) The creep wouldn't be noticeable until later, but to my mind it definitely started in the 90's.

In other words, the 90's was a very Plutonian decade. Of course, the influence of Pluto can be seen in other decades — the entire Cold War seems like a Plutonian phenomenon — but it was in the 90's that you just had this sickly-sweet smile on a rotting corpse.

1) Does this make sense? (Remember, astrology's not my strong suit. A divination I did in the past suggested that it's something I'll get to eventually, but I haven't yet.)

2) If so, was there anything going on with Pluto at the time that you (or anyone here) can think of that might have corresponded with this?

3) You've said that disasters discharge crud from the astral plane. Did 9/11 present us with a chance to discharge our karma — e.g. by recognizing that our empire and its ideology were harmful and unstable and backing out — and we blew it?

replies.title: Re: The Plutonian 90's
replies.content:
I have some thoughts about what was going on with Pluto in the 1990s.

1. Astronomers discovered the Kuiper belt. Pluto was in the spotlight. Throughout the decade, explorations continued, eventually resulting in the discovery (in 2005) of Eris, and the demotion (in 2006) of Pluto to a dwarf planet.

2. Between 1983-4 and 1995, Pluto was in Scorpio, a highly potent placement all by itself. (A web search using "Pluto in Scorpio generation" will turn up lots of viewpoints on this.)

3. Between 1989-1990 Saturn, Uranus and Neptune were conjunct in Capricorn, opposing Jupiter in Cancer, and sextile Pluto in Scorpio.

It makes sense to me that the 90s were strongly influenced by Plutonian forces, which potentiated the already tremendously powerful lineup of Saturn/Uranus/Neptune conjunct, with Jupiter in opposition acting as a cosmic magnifying glass.

replies.title: Re: The Plutonian 90's
replies.content:
Thanks for this! I was thinking it was the late 90's when things really come out, but that's makes its own kind of sense: Pluto and Scorpio are both about things happening in the background rather than out in the open, so having them together influencing an entire generation would amplify that. (In fact, since this placement is in most Millennials' birth charts, it might speak the concerns about privacy many of us are struggling with.)

So having things really start to come into the open and mainstream in the late 90's, after Pluto leaves the sign, makes a good bit of sense.

Dear JMG: You have sometimes mentioned that Frank Baum, the author of The Wizard of Oz, had a background in esoterism that shows up in his writing.

replies.title: Re: Frank Baum's Sources
replies.content:
Some of that is in the Oz books may well be Baum's own personal elaboration of what he learned from Theosophical amd related writings. The Emerald City is the green heart of a magical land surrounded by a Deadly Desert that kills whatever sets foot on it. The four quarters have their own colors, too: yellow in the East, red in the South, blue in the West, and purple in the North. I take these as sunrise, noonday, twilight, and gloom of night. (Whether F L Baum was also a Freemason I don't happen to know, but it seems likely from his circumstances. Anyway, Masonic lodge rooms reflect the same progress of the sun through the heavens.)

It's well documented that Baum, his wife, and especially his mother-in-law were members of the Theosophical Society. (It was his mother-in-law, Matilda Joslyn Gage, who first popularized the idea that the witch-hunts were actually launched against capable and knowledgeable women whose power and wisdom made men feel threatened. She was by far the most radical and esoterically-minded woman among the first-wave feminists in the USA, and her writings repay careful reading even today.)

replies.content:
Dear Robert M & JMG

I find that the Emerald City is a very specific thing in Sufi symbolism, and I wonder if it is just a powerful archetype that just popped into his imagination, or whether he picked it up through the Rosicrucians or Theosophy.

According to the Sufis, one is as likely as the other!

replies.title: Re: Frank Baum's Sources
replies.content:
There is also a school of thought that sees financial commentary hidden in The Wizard of Oz. In the book, Dorothy has SILVER slippers (though the movie folks thought "ruby" would look better in color). She takes the "Yellow Brick Road" ("goldbricks"?) to Oz. The residents of Oz are laboring under a delusion, related to a false form of money that was well known to be green. (In what units do we measure precious metals? Oz's, of course.) Turning her back on "greenback" money, Dorothy uses silver slippers to return to the reality she trusts: agriculture (the Scarecrow), forest products (the Tin Woodman), and animals of the wilderness (the Cowardly Lion).

Lathechuck

Blake in particular -- well, he was an occultist, of course, but he also understood how to cast some very potent spells in his verse. There are passages in Jerusalem that to my mind rank among the most powerful incantations ever written.

One of the keynotes of magic is that if you imagine something intensely enough, it can become real. Fiction is one way to imagine something very, very intensely...

replies.title: Re: Poetry and magic
replies.content: Take the guides, fling them on the ground, and jump up and down on them, yelling like a banshee. (Unless they're library books, in which case return them promptly and in good condition.)  You don't learn poetry by reading guides. You learn poetry by reading lots of poetry, memorizing poems that you love, and writing lots of verse. The first thousand or so poems you write will probably be pretty awful, but that doesn't matter. Yeats's earliest poems were pretty crappy, too.

I was attempting to visualize a healthier relationship with alcohol. I began confronting the possibility that such a healthy relationship might not be achievable for me, felt a sense of mourning, and began honestly visualizing what sort of outcomes this lends itself to if that avenue were closed off to me.

It was at this point that the profound experience began. It felt like another presence joined me, and I was attuned to her (it felt like a feminine presence) emotions. The feeling seemed to be an overwhelming abundance of compassion. At times it felt like I was feeling compassion towards myself, at times it felt like this presence was feeling compassion towards me, but I was somehow also experiencing those emotions.

Self-compassion has been one of the themes I've been working on in (psychoanalytical / trauma-informed) therapy, and this seemed like a personal masterclass on what compassion truly is. My flaws were entirely open, but there wasn't a sense of shame. I remember at one point bringing my flaws to focus and feeling this other presence's sadness. There was no pity, shame, judgment, or trying to ignore the truth. It was a full acknowledgement of the challenges I have ahead of me, empathy towards it, and a little bit of "this is tough but don't forget your strengths." Afterwards all I could do was cry due to the catharsis.

First of all, thank you JMG for all the material you provide here! Last week there was discussion of "the egg" being broken. While you mentioned it can be a somewhat random process, I feel like your material has allowed me to be more receptive to and better process what's happening.

It seems to me this could be any number of things could be happening. This could just be a personal catharsis, but there's something that feels external about what happened. Part of me thinks I could be picking up on a new emotion as a "track in space." What I was exploring regarding alcohol led me to "click" into this new pattern. comments.title: Hope Diamond
comments.content:
Any thoughts on the Hope Diamond?

I happened to go down a reading rabbit hole about this gem, which is owned by the American public, and resides at the Smithsonian (they are not getting rid of it, BTW).

It was acquired by the Smithsonian in 1958, but was once owned by Louis XIV, passed down to Louis XVI, and then disappeared after the French Revolution. It reappeared in England, much smaller, then eventually was owned by the McLean family of Virginia, who donated it to the Smithsonian.

A few interesting facts: it fluoresces red. In white light it is a misty dark blue. The time frame from Louis XIV acquiring it to the fall of the French Monarchy was three generations. And here we are...the US has owned it for roughly three generations.

Is this a thing or no? Obviously, the US has had a few...rough patches since its acquisition.

AND if it is a thing, is it more of a physical mechanism or a spiritual mechanism, and is there a way to "shut if off"?

replies.title: Re: Hope Diamond
replies.content: I don't know. It's not something I've researched.

comments.title: when I was much younger
comments.content:
Decades ago, when I was senior year of high school I had a strange experience. It seemed like there was "something" in one of the upper corners of my bedroom, something malevolent. I thought of it as a "bad vibe" that lived in the corner of that room. It had not always been there. SO, I just decided to sleep in the living room on the couch every night, I never slept in that room again. After a few months I went away to college, not bothered again, until more than a year later, in a dorm at college, it was there. Not just there, but closer, came closer, right on top of me realy. At this point I do not remember if that happened just the once or more than once. I think it was a few times. Finally, I decided what to do and commanded it to "go away" and I was not bothered any more.

Is this common ? What was it possibly and any thoughts ?

replies.title: Re: when I was much younger
replies.content: It's quite common. You might want to read David Hufford's The Terror that Comes in the Night, which discusses a similar type of experience. It was probably a spirit of some kind, but of course at this remove I can't tell what kind.

replies.title: Re: when I was much younger
replies.content:
I am going to look into that, thanks. On first glance on a search and description of the book on good reads, I am now reminded, that yes, I couldnt move, was paralyzed unable to move or speak. I dont know how I was able to finally on that last visit speak up,push thru and tell it to go away.

I am going to have to read this book. -- Thanks

replies.title: Re: when I was much younger
replies.content: But, looking at people comments in the review, I was not asleep first.  I did not wake up to this, it happened before I ever fell asleep. BUt those are peoples comments, I will read the book to see what he says, likely different varients

replies.title: Re: when I was much younger
replies.content: That's why I said "a similar type of experience" and not "the same type of experience."

replies.title: Re: when I was much younger
replies.content: Very common. I have it all the time. It can happen as you go to sleep or as you wake up. Just plain old sleep paralysis. Some critter comes and bothers you for a while. I've had all sorts. Some weak and easily banished and some extremely powerful that I have even communicated with. The most bizarre time was the three witches from Macbeth sitting at the foot of my bed discussing fairies. Advice to anyone, wiggle your fingers and toes. It eventually wakes you up. And then perform banishing rituals. I found that when I sleep with my partner I am less bothered too.

replies.title: Re: when I was much younger
replies.content:
I used to have haggings that took that form-- it's weird that you mention the upper corner of your room. Definitely, some of my repeated experiences with hagging involved formless presences in the corners around the ceiling: intensely malevolent, and laughing at me. And yes, that was accompanied by sleep paralysis, difficulty breathing, and a continuing sense of malevolent presence for some time after fully waking.

As an adult, I learned how to cross myself and invoke the Trinity, in my mind while paralyzed. This made them go away, and eventually I stopped experiencing the sleep paralysis entirely. I know others here have found other ways of defeating them.
